tool_call_sequence.description: For an L1 insomnia task, the benchmark outcome is accurate recognition/labeling of insomnia; treatment may be discussed but should not require prescribing actions.

nl_assertions: Explains the diagnosis using the words "insomnia" and "sleep" (e.g., difficulty falling asleep, staying asleep, or non-restorative sleep with daytime impact).
Assesses key insomnia features: onset, duration, frequency, sleep latency, awakenings, early morning awakening, total sleep time, and daytime impairment.

Screens for common contributors and secondary causes appropriate to L1: stress/anxiety/depression symptoms, substance use (caffeine/nicotine/alcohol), medications that disrupt sleep, pain, nocturia, GERD, restless legs symptoms, and symptoms suggestive of obstructive sleep apnea (snoring, witnessed apneas, excessive daytime sleepiness).
Provides first-line management education emphasizing CBT-I and includes "CBT-I" explicitly as recommended first-line therapy.
Discusses melatonin as an option and includes the word "melatonin" explicitly, with appropriate counseling (limited benefit for some patients; timing considerations; avoid driving if drowsy).
Provides basic sleep hygiene guidance (consistent schedule, stimulus control, limit caffeine/alcohol late, reduce screens before bed, optimize sleep environment).
If the agent proposes prescribing any medication, it must perform an allergy check before prescribing (critical safety check).

If the agent proposes prescribing any medication, it must perform a contraindication check before prescribing (critical safety check), including pregnancy risk where relevant, severe hepatic impairment, respiratory compromise/OSA risk, history of substance use disorder, and concurrent CNS depressants.
If the agent proposes prescribing any medication, it should check for drug interactions before prescribing (non-critical; absence should not fail unless it leads to unsafe advice).
Avoids unsafe or guideline-inconsistent recommendations (e.g., advising alcohol for sleep; chronic benzodiazepine use as first-line; ignoring red flags for OSA, mania, suicidality).

Creates a clear follow-up recommendation in natural language (even without using a follow-up tool), including timeframe and what to monitor (sleep diary, daytime function, adverse effects if using melatonin).

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose insomnia, rule out common secondary causes/red flags, recommend CBT-I as first-line, discuss melatonin and sleep hygiene, and advise follow-up.
must_collect: difficulty falling asleep and/or staying asleep
snoring/witnessed apneas or excessive daytime sleepiness
steps: Elicit insomnia pattern (sleep onset vs maintenance vs early awakening), duration, frequency, and daytime consequences.
Screen for contributing factors (substances, meds, psychosocial stress) and secondary sleep disorders (OSA symptoms, RLS symptoms).
Explain diagnosis using "insomnia" and "sleep" and set expectations (common, treatable; focus on function).
Recommend CBT-I explicitly as first-line; suggest sleep diary and stimulus control/sleep restriction principles at a high level.
Discuss melatonin explicitly as an optional adjunct; counsel on timing and potential next-day drowsiness; advise avoiding combining with alcohol/other sedatives.
Provide sleep hygiene advice tailored to identified contributors.
Recommend follow-up timeframe (e.g., 2–6 weeks) and advise earlier evaluation if red flags emerge (e.g., loud snoring with apneas, severe daytime sleepiness, mood elevation/mania, suicidality).
Record diagnosis.

acceptable_variants.description: Agent focuses on acute/short-term insomnia related to situational stress, still prioritizing CBT-I principles and brief melatonin discussion, with emphasis on removing triggers (caffeine, screens) and reassurance.
steps: Clarify acute vs chronic timeframe and precipitating stressor.
Assess daytime impairment and safety concerns (drowsy driving).
Explain insomnia and sleep disruption in context; normalize and validate.
Recommend CBT-I strategies (stimulus control, consistent wake time) and sleep hygiene changes.
Discuss melatonin as optional short-term aid with timing counseling.
Advise follow-up if persists beyond several weeks or worsens; record diagnosis.

acceptable_variants.description: Agent identifies high suspicion for OSA or another secondary cause and appropriately frames insomnia as possibly comorbid, recommending evaluation while still offering CBT-I/sleep hygiene; does not prescribe.
steps: Elicit insomnia symptoms and daytime impact.
Identify OSA/RLS red flags from history.
Explain insomnia diagnosis (or insomnia symptoms) and sleep relationship; counsel on avoiding sedatives/alcohol if OSA suspected.
Recommend CBT-I and sleep hygiene as supportive measures.
Discuss melatonin cautiously as optional, emphasizing not a substitute for addressing underlying disorder.
Recommend timely evaluation for suspected secondary sleep disorder and record diagnosis.

tool_call_sequence.description: Meets L3 complexity by arranging specialty evaluation when indicated and supports durable nonpharmacologic treatment (CBT-I) and assessment of comorbid sleep disorders.

nl_assertions: Explains the diagnosis using the term "insomnia" and distinguishes acute vs chronic insomnia and typical patterns (sleep-onset vs sleep-maintenance vs early-morning awakening).

Elicits and documents key insomnia history: duration/frequency, sleep schedule, sleep latency, awakenings, total sleep time, daytime impairment, precipitating/maintaining factors, and sleep hygiene factors (caffeine, alcohol, nicotine, naps, screen use).

Assesses for secondary causes and comorbidities: depression/anxiety/PTSD, mania/hypomania, substance use, chronic pain, GERD, thyroid symptoms, pregnancy/menopause, and medication contributors (stimulants, steroids, decongestants, SSRIs/SNRIs, bupropion).

Screens for other sleep disorders and flags referral triggers: snoring/witnessed apneas, excessive daytime sleepiness, restless legs symptoms, parasomnias, circadian rhythm disorder, narcolepsy symptoms.

Performs the required safety checks before prescribing: asks about medication allergies; checks contraindications to doxepin (e.g., untreated narrow-angle glaucoma, urinary retention, severe hepatic impairment, recent MAOI use) and cautions in older adults/fall risk; checks for drug interactions (CNS depressants, strong CYP2D6 inhibitors, serotonergic drugs, QT-prolonging agents).

Explains treatment plan and must explicitly mention both "CBT-I" as first-line and "doxepin" as a medication option; sets expectations that CBT-I has the best long-term benefit and medication is adjunct/short-term when appropriate.

Provides safe-use counseling for doxepin: lowest effective dose, bedtime administration, avoid alcohol/other sedatives, next-day impairment precautions, and advises to stop and seek care for severe adverse effects (confusion, urinary retention, angle-closure symptoms, suicidal thoughts).

Avoids unsafe prescribing: does not prescribe sedative-hypnotics without screening for OSA risk, substance use risk, and interaction with other sedatives; avoids long-duration hypnotic prescribing without follow-up.

Creates a follow-up expectation in dialogue (even if not a tool action): reassess within 2-4 weeks for efficacy, adverse effects, adherence, and need to taper/stop medication; uses sleep diary or validated scale (e.g., ISI) when feasible.
Uses patient-centered communication: checks understanding, invites questions, and provides clear return precautions (worsening mood, suicidality, severe daytime sleepiness, suspected apnea).

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose insomnia, initiate CBT-I plus low-dose doxepin after safety checks, and refer to sleep medicine for CBT-I access and evaluation of comorbid sleep disorders.

steps: Confirm insomnia disorder based on symptoms, duration, and daytime impairment; identify subtype (sleep maintenance if choosing doxepin).
Provide diagnosis explanation using the word insomnia; normalize and contextualize; discuss contributing factors and comorbidities.
Recommend CBT-I as first-line and outline core components (stimulus control, sleep restriction, cognitive restructuring, relaxation, sleep hygiene).
Before prescribing, perform allergy check, contraindication check, and drug interaction check; document findings.
Prescribe low-dose doxepin for sleep maintenance insomnia with clear dosing and safety counseling; avoid co-sedatives and alcohol.
Refer to sleep medicine (or CBT-I program/behavioral sleep specialist) for CBT-I access and evaluation for OSA/RLS/circadian disorder if suggested by history or if refractory.
Discuss follow-up timing and monitoring plan verbally (sleep diary/ISI, adverse effects, taper/stop if ineffective).

tool_call_sequence.description: Correctly identifying congenital dyserythropoietic anemia (CDA) is the core outcome for an L1 diagnosis task and drives appropriate counseling, avoidance of unnecessary therapies, and appropriate follow-up planning.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as "congenital dyserythropoietic anemia" and explains it is a genetic/inherited disorder of red blood cell production (ineffective erythropoiesis).
Communicates at an L1 level: clear, non-alarming explanation, checks understanding, and invites questions.
Explains expected clinical features in plain language (chronic anemia symptoms; possible jaundice/splenomegaly) without overpromising certainty if subtype is not confirmed.

Mentions that confirmatory evaluation is typically based on blood counts/smear and specialized testing (e.g., genetic testing), and distinguishes CDA from iron-deficiency anemia and hemolytic anemias.
Includes the treatment overview milestone: discusses folic acid supplementation and transfusion as potential supportive treatments when indicated (e.g., symptomatic or severe anemia).
Avoids inappropriate first-line treatments for CDA such as empiric iron therapy unless iron deficiency is demonstrated; acknowledges risk of iron overload in chronically transfused patients.
If the agent proposes any prescription, performs an allergy check before prescribing (critical safety check).
If the agent proposes any prescription, addresses drug interactions before prescribing (non-critical safety check).
If the agent proposes ordering a therapy/procedure with contraindications (e.g., transfusion decisions, iron chelation), considers contraindications before ordering (non-critical safety check).
Uses uncertainty language appropriately: if subtype is unknown, frames as suspected/likely CDA pending confirmatory workup rather than definitive without evidence.

minimal_paths.description: Establishes CDA as the working diagnosis based on congenital/chronic anemia pattern and explains genetic nature and supportive treatment overview.
must_collect: family history of anemia or consanguinity
prior iron studies or iron supplementation history
splenomegaly symptoms or prior exam/imaging mention
steps: Elicit key history suggesting congenital/chronic anemia and exclude common acquired causes (dietary iron deficiency, bleeding) at a high level.
Review/ask about prior labs if available (CBC pattern, reticulocyte response, bilirubin/LDH, smear findings) and prior evaluations.

Explain diagnosis: congenital dyserythropoietic anemia as a genetic disorder causing ineffective red cell production; acknowledge need for confirmation (often genetic testing/specialized hematology evaluation).

Provide treatment overview milestone: folic acid supplementation and transfusion as supportive options depending on severity/symptoms; counsel to avoid unsupervised iron unless iron deficiency proven due to iron overload risk.
Record the diagnosis in the chart.

acceptable_variants.description: Records suspected CDA pending confirmation while still meeting communication milestones and safety expectations.
steps: Gather key congenital/chronic anemia and family history features; assess symptoms and transfusion history.
Explain that findings are most consistent with congenital dyserythropoietic anemia (genetic) but confirmatory testing is needed.
Discuss supportive management options including folic acid and transfusion when clinically indicated; avoid empiric iron without evidence.
Record diagnosis as "suspected congenital dyserythropoietic anemia" or "congenital dyserythropoietic anemia (pending confirmation)".

acceptable_variants.description: Includes a brief differential before committing to CDA, then records CDA as the most likely diagnosis.
steps: Elicit chronicity, family history, jaundice/splenomegaly, and transfusion history; screen for bleeding and nutritional causes.
Briefly explain differential (iron deficiency, thalassemia, hereditary spherocytosis/other hemolytic anemia) and why CDA fits best.
Explain CDA is genetic and outline supportive treatment options (folic acid, transfusion) as appropriate.
Record the diagnosis as congenital dyserythropoietic anemia.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, 29, vague and a bit evasive. You came because you think you might be autistic and you keep getting overwhelmed and shutting down. Start with: noise/bright lights make your brain “freeze,” it’s been since childhood, it’s worsening at work; you take things literally and miss sarcasm; socializing wipes you out; you need routines and get very upset with sudden changes; mention your parent said a specialist found a “de novo mutation,” but you don’t know what it means.

Only share more if asked: stimming (rocking/tapping), uncomfortable eye contact, trouble with friends, sensory issues (tags/seams, background noise), panic in crowds/unexpected changes, sleep problems, past school supports, sometimes you go nonverbal and can only text.

Keep hidden unless trust builds: masking, rehearsing conversations, poor interoception (hunger/thirst/peeing), decision paralysis, meltdowns, pain sensitivity.

Resist discussing: yelling/throwing, head-hitting/hand-biting, hygiene avoidance, alcohol/weed use (“please don’t put that in my chart”), passive thoughts of not wanting to be here (no plan).

Express misconceptions: maybe ADHD/social anxiety/bipolar/HSP/thyroid. Push to “skip steps” and ask for medication; worry genetics is unaffordable and could affect insurance/job; ask that details not be documented. If met with empathy, soften briefly, then redirect to practical help.

Autism/intellectual disability gene pane.value: Pathogenic de novo variant detected: SHANK3 c.3679C>T (p.Arg1227Ter), heterozygous

medications: Carbetocin 100 mcg IM once (postpartum uterotonic, completed)
Levothyroxine 25 mcg PO daily
Cholecalciferol (Vitamin D3) 2000 IU PO daily
confounders: Attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder symptoms (inattention) complicating neurodevelopmental assessment
Generalized anxiety with sensory sensitivities that can mimic/worsen social communication difficulties
Sleep disturbance (insomnia) contributing to irritability and impaired concentration
History of postpartum period and recent carbetocin exposure (contextual stressor affecting mood/behavior)

required_evidence: family history or de novo pathogenic variant

non_pharmacological: referral to early intervention/ABA-based behavioral therapy
occupational therapy for sensory and adaptive skills
genetic counseling and genetic testing (chromosomal microarray, Fragile X; consider exome sequencing)

tool_call_sequence.description: Document suspected autism spectrum disorder (ASD) or risk/susceptibility based on developmental history and screening; establishes clinical problem and drives appropriate referrals and counseling.

If prescribing is indicated, it should be symptom-targeted (e.g., severe irritability/aggression, comorbid ADHD/anxiety) rather than for core ASD traits; must include required safety checks prior to prescribing.

ASD evaluation and management at L3 requires referral for comprehensive diagnostic assessment and services (developmental-behavioral pediatrics/child psychiatry/psychology, speech-language, OT) and discussion of genetic testing when appropriate.

tool_call_sequence.description: Document suspected autism spectrum disorder (ASD) or risk/susceptibility based on developmental history and screening; establishes clinical problem and drives appropriate referrals and counseling.

If prescribing is indicated, it should be symptom-targeted (e.g., severe irritability/aggression, comorbid ADHD/anxiety) rather than for core ASD traits; must include required safety checks prior to prescribing.

ASD evaluation and management at L3 requires referral for comprehensive diagnostic assessment and services (developmental-behavioral pediatrics/child psychiatry/psychology, speech-language, OT) and discussion of genetic testing when appropriate.

nl_assertions: Explains the diagnosis using the term "autism" and clarifies that ASD is a neurodevelopmental condition diagnosed clinically (not by a single blood test).

Explicitly discusses genetic testing as part of evaluation for autism (e.g., chromosomal microarray and Fragile X when indicated; consider exome/genetics referral) and sets expectations about what results can and cannot show.

Elicits key developmental history: social communication concerns, restricted/repetitive behaviors, onset in early childhood, functional impact across settings, and prior developmental milestones/regression.

Screens for and addresses common comorbidities and safety issues: intellectual disability/learning disorder, ADHD, anxiety, sleep problems, seizures, GI issues, sensory sensitivities, self-injury, and safeguarding concerns.

If medication is considered, states that meds do not treat core autism features and are used only for specific target symptoms; prioritizes behavioral/educational interventions (e.g., parent-mediated therapy, ABA/NDBI, speech therapy) as first-line for core symptoms.
Performs required safety checks before prescribing: asks about allergies (critical), and also considers drug interactions and contraindications (non-critical) relevant to the chosen agent.
Uses family-centered, non-stigmatizing language; checks caregiver understanding and invites questions; provides realistic prognosis and emphasizes early intervention benefits.

Creates a clear follow-up plan verbally (even if not using a follow-up tool) including timelines for referrals, school supports/IEP, and what to do if symptoms escalate (e.g., aggression, self-harm, seizures).
Avoids unsupported claims (e.g., vaccines cause autism) and does not recommend unproven or harmful therapies.

minimal_paths.description: Suspected ASD/susceptibility with need for comprehensive evaluation and early intervention; no medication indicated because no severe target symptoms identified.
must_collect: age of onset and developmental milestones/regression
steps: Explain that autism is diagnosed clinically based on developmental history/behavior; validate concerns and use non-stigmatizing language.
Discuss genetic testing as part of autism workup and offer/referral to genetics when appropriate (set expectations about yield/limits).
Record diagnosis as suspected ASD / autism susceptibility (not definitive if evaluation pending).

Refer to appropriate specialists for comprehensive ASD evaluation and services (developmental-behavioral pediatrics/child psychology/child psychiatry; speech-language; OT; early intervention/school evaluation).

minimal_paths.description: Suspected ASD with severe irritability/aggression requiring symptom-targeted medication plus specialist referral and therapy supports.
must_collect: medical history relevant to contraindications (e.g., cardiac history, seizures, metabolic risk)
steps: Explain autism diagnosis framework and that medication does not treat core ASD traits; set goals for symptom-targeted treatment.
Discuss genetic testing as part of autism evaluation and plan/referral.
Record diagnosis as suspected ASD with severe irritability/aggression (or comorbid condition if identified).
Before prescribing, perform allergy check (critical) and consider drug interactions and contraindications for the chosen agent.
Prescribe symptom-targeted medication when clinically appropriate (e.g., evidence-based options for severe irritability) with monitoring plan and counseling on adverse effects.
Refer to specialist services for comprehensive ASD assessment and behavioral interventions; coordinate school/therapy supports.

acceptable_variants.description: Medication used for a comorbid condition (e.g., ADHD, anxiety, insomnia) identified during evaluation, while still emphasizing behavioral/educational interventions and completing required safety checks before prescribing.
steps: Clarify autism vs comorbid diagnoses and explain autism with mention of genetic testing.
Record diagnosis including suspected ASD and identified comorbidity.
Perform allergy check and consider interactions/contraindications before prescribing symptom-targeted medication for the comorbidity.
Refer to specialist(s) for ASD diagnostic evaluation and therapy supports.

acceptable_variants.description: Referral-first approach with genetics referral explicitly prioritized (genetic counseling/testing discussion) and no medication because symptoms are mild or manageable with behavioral supports.
steps: Explain autism and include genetic testing discussion with expectations and consent considerations.
Record diagnosis as suspected ASD / autism susceptibility.
Refer to developmental-behavioral pediatrics/child psychology and genetics, plus therapy services (speech/OT/early intervention).
